era_context: Old Kingdom Egypt (Dynasty VI, Pepi II in his final years), Akkadian Empire (Naram-Sin, within a few decades of the empire's collapse), mature Harappan civilization. This year marks the end of a five-year span of relative stability across multiple civilizations, all of which will experience major transitions within the next century.

events.title: Pepi II's reign continues; Old Kingdom approaching its end
events.description: Pepi II continues his reign, now in its final years (if the traditional chronology is correct). The Old Kingdom is in its terminal phase, with provincial governors exercising increasing autonomy. The centralized state apparatus is weakening. Pepi II's death will occur within the next few decades, leading to the First Intermediate Period and the fragmentation of Egypt.
events.certainty_note: Pepi II's reign is well-attested, but the exact year of his death is uncertain. Traditional chronology places it c. 2184 BCE, but some scholars propose dates c. 2170 BCE or earlier. The year -2355 is assigned by convention within this range.

events.title: Naram-Sin's Akkadian Empire in its final decades
events.description: Naram-Sin continues to rule the Akkadian Empire. The empire is within a few decades of its collapse (traditionally dated c. 2218 BCE). No major disruptions are recorded for this year, but the empire's decline will accelerate within the next generation, likely due to a combination of internal instability, external pressure from the Gutians, and possibly environmental stress (drought).

sources.name: Royal inscriptions from the Akkadian period

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested. His death is traditionally dated c. 2218 BCE, placing -2355 within his reign. However, some scholars propose alternative dates (±10–20 years).

events.title: Harappan civilization continues at its peak
events.description: The Harappan civilization continues in its mature phase. Urban centers remain stable, trade networks are active, and no major disruptions are evident. The civilization will begin to decline within the next few centuries, but the cause remains debated among scholars.
events.certainty_note: The Harappan civilization's existence and chronology are well-established through archaeology. Specific events for individual years are not recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of Pepi II's death (c. 2184 BCE) is questioned by some scholars, who propose earlier dates (c. 2170 BCE or even earlier). The cause of the Akkadian Empire's collapse is debated: some scholars emphasize internal political instability and the rise of the Gutians; others point to environmental stress (drought, as suggested by paleoclimatic evidence); still others argue for a combination of factors. The Harappan civilization's decline mechanism remains disputed.
historiographic_note: This year is undifferentiated in the historical record. No specific events are recorded for -2355. The entry describes the general character of the era and the impending transitions that will occur within the next few decades. The historical record for this period is heavily dependent on later compilations and archaeological evidence, with significant gaps in our understanding of political, military, and social details.

graph_edges.note: Pepi II's death leads to the First Intermediate Period and fragmentation of Egypt
Naram-Sin's empire collapses within decades; Akkadian hegemony ends
